speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Paul Wos. Would you describe to me how you went out to get the gold teeth from the corpses in Flossenbuerg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was practice, the old people who die was-- the dress was take out. And now get the body was store, in one place, close to the crematorium. And [BACKGROUND NOISE] it happened was behind my place, where I sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, this cell number-- the room number four in hospital. And one day-- you like to know the story? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, during night, some prisoner-- that was, sort of, later there, we know a Russian officer-- go to the room during night and where was dark. This was against the law, local law. And go there and try pick up the gold teeth. Later, they can do something with these, trade with-- for food or something. And tower, watchtower was very close there. And the watchmen has the reflector. Put the reflector and saw somebody is there, start shooting from automatic pistol. And next day, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I know was shooting. And we was only couple feet from the dead body stack. And thank [BACKGROUND NOISE] dead body we was survive. If was less dead body, for example, 1-foot not 10-foot high or 5-foot high, probably I was-- I'd be dead. And the fellow, who was in the-- try stole the teeth, disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead body was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sent later there, each day, sent to crematorium. And two fellows take the how you going to-- take two dead body and go, with the SS man, to crematorium. And mostly, it was 10, 20 guys carting, all day, the body to crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you collect the gold teeth from the dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not me. That was special job for doctor, who-- a French doctor, who collect the teeth and mark special sign, this body is ready to go to crematorium. Now, this French doctor, later there, is supposed to give back the gold to the SS people. And the SS people, you know, doing what supposed to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated, if I remember it exactly, 23rd March-- May, 1945, by American Army 97 and later the-- excuse me, the 97 and 90 division, the General Patton Third Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like? I saw 1,000 different films. And I never saw this situation, condition, emotion like I saw this time. At this moment, I was on the post, where was the entrance to the camp. And the-- the people was so emotional, kiss the foot, hand of the American boys. And for us, it was-- everybody crying. And it was a very emotional situation, very emotional. I never saw in any film, in my life, like this was emotional situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a warning that they were coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: A warning? It was-- you see, we was again lucky. Why? I am talking about the camp was 30,000 people, prisoners. And on lazaret, mean hospital, was about 1,200, with the people like we who are to help. And all camp go to death march. And we, hospital, stay. And next day supposed to come back, SS, and shoot us. And was already between the people who left there, I mean in hospital, was prepared some-- some action.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was not involved in this action, because I was not so long there. And what happened? American boys liberate us and start shooting maybe two hours before came to our camp. Start shooting and German not come back again. We was liberated. How many people do you estimate were there, altogether, who were liberated right at Flossenbuerg?

speaker: interviewee
text: About over 1,000 people, over 1,000. Only this-- the liberation was for some people very good, for some people was death, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because American boys give everything what was in pocket to eat, between this chocolate. And how many people eat chocolate, eat whole piece and next day die? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not so bad condition. I was during the working in hospital, I was very bad condition. I was 47 kilogram weight, only. I go through typhus. And before our liberation, I pick up, you know, a little bit of body. And I have enough to eat not die, because all the time I was in hospital a little bit of more soup. And I was there prepare a little bit better. And after American went there, I not eat. Because my friend said, the doctor, the Polish doctor said don't eat too much fat thing. I mean chocolate, too. And thus, we tried to do this. And that we-- we survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was normally your weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weight? I [LAUGHS] 70 pounds, 70% of this what your regular, 50% of what your regular weight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were 47 kilos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would your normal weight have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal amount of weight? I tell you what was was 65 kilogram. In other words, 65, 47, something like this. 68.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we was liberated, I was sent, right away, by American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unit, to the new camp, where we-- I work for-- for American hospital, again, doing the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, couple times, was not the guy who give the narcos for American guys or for other. I give the narcos. What kind narcos was? Mask and couple of the ether. That was so-called narcos.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the story. Later there, I first transport to Poland. I go to Poland and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] check my sister and mother are, here, in Poland. Because I know, by radio, are in Poland. And I was hoping all three person can go with me through the green-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not legal way to West. And my mother said, no, I am too sick go there. And I am too tired go there to American zone, and my sister the same. And I go, myself, back. Was-- because I go not legal, was shooting and something like this. Now, still, I go through the border. And I meet, again, my father, who was find my aunt in Hohenfels-- Hohenfels, near Regensburg. And my father, next transport, took everything and my-- my aunt and go to Poland. I stayed there for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Regensburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Regensburg. And you know, I travel a lot for a special transport company, like military guy, and France and Germany. And after one year, I come back to Poland and start problem, for me, political problem. Because two time, I have the same paper cross the border one way. It mean something wrong. And was all right, you know, because it was a different situation. And after I come back, I meet [? my ?] father. I work, with father, with small factory, what my father pick up from the basement some stuff. And later there, I go to Polytechnical Institute, finish my degree. And after I finish my degree, because I have the best mark in my engineering way, I was given, by state, permission pick up office what you want. And I pick up, in Warsaw, water-- one office in whole state water power station. We designed water power station. That's my profession. And I work there x-number years. 1960, we intend to go to United State, because my friend working in Florida, also engineer from Switzerland, prepare for me first preference to this company. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very good. I was first engineer go out of the Poland, with the family, legally. I was wait-- waiting 15 years. And I go away. And I start here. And you know, in-- I supposed to go to Florida.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later there, my brother, a political refugee from-- go to United States from Paris, from France. And we-- we keep together, not split. And I ask this company, you maybe you release me from this obligation? The company, oh, if his situation like this-- you know American hearts-- [LAUGHS] we ask for other guy. And from this moment, I start here a small office, small design, smaller innovation, a small construction company. You know, all the time be own boss, working hard, sometime 12 hours daily, because some hours in the office, some time in the field, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was opportunity to give chance, give education, my children, what for-- for me very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of construction did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I design, for example-- first, I work for, here, you know, North Shore Hospital. You know that one? That was the third-- the couple buildings, that's my job, from beginning to the top.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other, if you cross the Hempstead Turnpike, Hofstra University, you know the bridge, funny bridge? That's my personally job. [LAUGHS] You know, that tall, second, library, the tallest building on Long Island, near the bridge, library, that's my job, from foundation to the top, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later there, I quit the company, because I supposed to go to coliseum. You know, on across-- this is coliseum. I supposed to work there like engineer. Only, my boss was Italian. And some Italian guy applied, too. My boss took the guy, [LAUGHS] Italian. From this moment, I said, oh my goodness, I not like-- like-- like this. And I start my own business, you know, and doing some renovation, doing drawing for somebody, something like this, you know. And was successful from beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I am 60-- 78-- 76 years old. It is a little bit difficult, you know, not doing nothing, you know? And we live only on this what we little bit save, and what we have some building, collect the rent, you know, something like that. And now it is very bad situation. Still, you know, it's better like in concentration camp. [LAUGHS] When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I came back to Poland from Germany, I meet my wife. Because, during the war, father-- my-- my-- my wife father was working for my father, like mechanic, and was very nice person. And later, thereafter, I come back to Poland, my wife-- my mother says, I have for you the girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I not like married. [LAUGHS] And we meet together. And, you know, probably I did the best in my life, best movement in my life, because maybe the other girl be not so patient like was my wife? How long you've been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 49 years. Next year, be 50, if I still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two very good children. And I wish everybody has this kind children. Konrad is 42 years old or 45. My daughter is 40. For example, my daughter has two children, married, two twins. Konrad has two girls. Konrad is architect. My daughter is a physician. And, you know, are good children, good children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's God give me something back. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What has your war experiences taught you about life and the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your war experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What lesson has it given you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, lesson? You see, how many times I see something what I can help people, I try to do this. Why? Because I know this is boomerang go back. You help. You be help. That's my-- and still, you know, is too many people animal, like right now [? with ?] Bosnia, Herzegovina, Chechnya, and something like this. That's-- I am against the totalisms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I not belong to any party, no party. Because, during the time I was in Poland, was only really one party, Communist party. And I know what this party did for people, how trick the people, how work for NKVD, for Soviet, you know? I not trust party people. That's my feeling. Maybe I am wrong. You ask me, you are Democrat? No, I not belong to any party. I am not Democrat, not Republican, not no. I feel-- I trust people who are people, you know? Who are animal or something what, you know, only my pocket is important, the other people can die, important matter, for me are not-- not people. That's-- that's that's something wrong. What message would you like to leave for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I can I don't know I can leave some message my children. Because I learn sometime from my children, you believe? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you learned from your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Honesty, good heart, [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sharing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you think they learned that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Maybe from my wife. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to introduce my wife Wanda. We married over 47-- 49 years. That's my daughter, Aldona, my son, Konrad, my two grandchildren, Taylor, Carson, Anya, and Andrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we happy having so beautiful family here after 49 and half years-- correct, [LAUGHS] that. And because the best thing what happen, have a beautiful [BACKGROUND NOISE] grandchildren and living in free country.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are delighted to be able to take this opportunity to look back and to see what my father, his parents, my mother, what they [BACKGROUND NOISE] accomplished during that time. I am absolutely excited about their believing to survive through the war. It is a complete testimony to their views about helping people and about tolerance for all people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I especially would like to thank my father and, of course, my mother, because everything that we have learned in reference to respecting other people, besides our-- our immediate family, of course, we learned from them. And they taught it to us based on their experiences during the war years. So all their love and kindness towards us is the same that they have given to other people. And we hope that we, in turn, can give it to our children, our children to their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say that I'm really glad that I'm here today because of my grandfather. And it's just really nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I love my grandfather and grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my parents, Pawel Wos and Anna Wos. Is from house [NON-ENGLISH]. This photo we pick up-- not we, my sister pick up from the building where we was living before the Warsaw Uprising, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken probably 1920-- 1920.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my picture. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] chris? Confirmation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --confirmation. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's was 1920-- maybe '08, something like this. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's picture of my sister, Irina, on left. In the middle, that's other sister, Wanda, and also my brother, Odon. That's probably 1930-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1931. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That's my picture. I was working for American Army, 90 Division. And that was 19-- 19-- 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances of working for the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, after liberation, I voluntary work for people who liberate me. That's the picture from Batory, big ship, take us from Gdynia, Polish port, to Montreal, to Canada. That was-- on left is my wife, Konrad, Aldona, and also my picture, 1961 going to United State. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture represent all my family during this time. I was decorated by consul general in-- in New York, a couple medals during the war and also from concentration camp, from Auschwitz. That's all my family and friends. This picture represents my speech in Barnes and Noble during the author sign a book about the Catholic in Poland save the Jewish people. And on left side is the author. And in funny position, I am here. And was couple hundred people, and, you know, was a very nice meeting. Mostly it was Jewish people from Great Neck and this area and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] give me a-- a standing ovation. This represent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] death camp oven, where the people was burn and the crematorium, other words. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I was visit after 50 years liberation. I was in my Flossenbuerg concentration camp. And that's the picture and many scarves are from people who was at this time there. This is original my number from concentration camp Flossenbuerg. My number was 23504. My father was next number, 505. This is the-- I was invite by 90 Division reunion, each year, people who liberate me from the concentration camp. And I was-- after 50 years, I was able [? thank ?] these people who was, for me, you know, angels. This is document from Holocaust Museum in Washington, DC. And I was recognized like, on this picture, and to the-- a sentence here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does is describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was helping, you know? And I was-- I mean I was helping Jewish people. And that was my recognition. This book is history of the family Wos, [BACKGROUND NOISE] by Paul Wos, Irena Wos Madjiski, Odon Wos, Wanda Lorenc. The sentence is inside. 'Each man fight his battle confirming his own adversary at his own time and in his own way. This book was written to bring to light the qualities of character that help a generation to win its battles. May it give to those yet to came the courage and the believing to win theirs.' These medals was given me by Polish government after this what I did during the 1939-1945.
